resources.string: How to measure
How to measure
Analysis result guide
Analysis Result Guide
Body Fat % (Male)
Body Fat % (Female)
Waist-Hip Ratio (Male)
Waist-Hip Ratio (Female)
Is associated with very low body weight and increased risk of weakness, low energy, and impaired immune function. BMI does not account for muscle mass or fat distribution, though.
Is associated with low body weight and may increase the risk of fatigue, nutrient deficiencies, and reduced physical resilience. BMI does not account for muscle mass or fat distribution, though.
Is associated with a healthy weight range and lower risk of weight-related health problems. BMI does not account for muscle mass or fat distribution, though.
Is associated with increased body weight and may increase health risks over time, especially for heart and metabolic health. BMI does not account for muscle mass or fat distribution, though.
Is associated with higher body weight and increased risk of conditions such as heart disease and type 2 diabetes. BMI does not account for muscle mass or fat distribution, though.
Is associated with significantly increased body weight and a higher likelihood of serious health conditions. BMI does not account for muscle mass or fat distribution, though.
Is associated with very high body weight and a substantially increased risk of severe health complications. BMI does not account for muscle mass or fat distribution, though.
Is associated with extremely low body fat and increased risk of hormonal imbalance and impaired body function. However, body fat percentage alone does not take fat distribution into account.
Is associated with very low body fat and may reduce energy availability and physical resilience. However, body fat percentage alone does not take fat distribution into account.
Is associated with low body fat and is commonly seen in physically trained individuals.
Is associated with a healthy level of body fat and good overall physical condition.
Is associated with a typical body fat range and mild to moderate health risk.
Is associated with high body fat and increased risk of metabolic and cardiovascular conditions. However, body fat percentage alone does not take fat distribution into account.
Is associated with extremely low body fat and increased risk of hormonal disruption and menstrual irregularities. However, body fat percentage alone does not take fat distribution into account.
Is associated with very low body fat and may impact hormone balance and energy levels. However, body fat percentage alone does not take fat distribution into account.
Is associated with low body fat and is commonly seen in physically trained individuals.
Is associated with a healthy and sustainable body fat level.
Is associated with a typical body fat range and mild to moderate health risk.
Is associated with high body fat and increased risk of chronic health conditions. However, body fat percentage alone does not take fat distribution into account.
Is associated with lower abdominal fat and reduced risk of cardiovascular disease.
Is associated with moderate abdominal fat and mild increase in health risk.
Is associated with higher abdominal fat and increased risk of heart and metabolic conditions.
Is associated with high abdominal fat and a significantly increased risk of serious health conditions.
Is associated with lower abdominal fat and reduced health risk.
Is associated with moderate abdominal fat and mild increase in health risk.
Is associated with increased abdominal fat and higher risk of metabolic and cardiovascular conditions.
Is associated with high abdominal fat and a significantly increased risk of serious health issues.
Is associated with a low waist size relative to height and might indicate low body fat or underweight status. However, health risk cannot be determined from this measure alone.
Is associated with a healthy waist size and lower risk of metabolic disease.
Is associated with increased abdominal fat and mild to moderate health risk.
Is associated with high abdominal fat and increased likelihood of health problems.
Is associated with very high abdominal fat and a strong link to serious health risks.
Show measurement guidance
Front
Back
M
F

Weigh yourself at the same time of day (ideally in the morning after getting up and after using the toilet) and without clothing or with very light clothing. Stand centered on the scale and remain still until the reading is stable.

Use a body fat scale and make sure you stand barefoot with clean, dry feet on the electrodes. Measure under the same conditions whenever possible (time of day, hydration), as the reading is strongly influenced by these factors.

Look straight ahead and relax your shoulders. Measure the neck circumference directly below the larynx (Adam's apple). Keep the measuring tape horizontal.

⚤ Difference by sex:
♂ Men: directly below the Adam's apple
♀ Women: at the narrowest point of the neck (if the Adam's apple is not clearly visible)

Stand upright and breathe normally - measure at the end of a normal exhale. The measuring tape should be horizontal.

⚤ Difference by sex:
♂ Men: circumference at nipple level
♀ Women: circumference at the fullest part of the chest; ideally wear an unpadded bra or no bra

Find the lowest palpable rib of your rib cage and the highest point of your hip bone. Stand relaxed and breathe out normally. Measure the circumference midway between the rib cage and hip. Keep the measuring tape horizontal and do not pull it too tight.

Stand upright and relax your abdomen. Measure the circumference directly at navel level. The measuring tape should be horizontal.\n\n⚠️ Note: This measurement differs from the waist circumference - it is always taken at the navel.
Stand upright with feet together. Measure the circumference at the widest point of the hips and buttocks. Make sure the measuring tape remains horizontal.

Grasp the skin midway between the nipple and the fold that forms between the arm and chest when the arm hangs down. Grasp so that the measured skin fold runs from the nipple toward the armpit.

⚠️ Important: The orientation of the fold matters! It is taken diagonally.

⚤ Difference by sex:
♂ Men: measure as described
♀ Women: optional measurement - not required for the 3-point body fat analysis.

Grasp the skin approx. 2-3 cm to the right of the navel.

⚠️ Important: The orientation of the fold matters! It is taken vertically.

⚤ Difference by sex:
♂ Men: measure as described
♀ Women: optional measurement - not required for the 3-point body fat analysis.

Stand relaxed and shift your weight to the other leg. Grasp the skin on the front of the thigh, approximately midway between the hip and the knee.\n\n⚠️ Important: The orientation of the fold matters! It is taken vertically.

Let the arm hang loosely. Grasp the skin on the back of the upper arm, midway between the shoulder and elbow.

⚠️ Important: The orientation of the fold matters! It is taken vertically.

⚤ Difference by sex:
♀ Women: measure as described
♂ Men: optional measurement - not required for the 3-point body fat analysis.

Grasp the skin directly above the hip bone, running slightly diagonally downward toward the front.

⚠️ Important: The orientation of the fold matters! It is taken diagonally.

⚤ Difference by sex:
♀ Women: measure as described
♂ Men: optional measurement - not required for the 3-point body fat analysis.
